readingLong.title: The Hidden Cost of 'Free' Apps
readingLong.passage:
Open the app store on any phone and you will find millions of programs that cost nothing to download. Games, social networks, photo editors, and messaging tools are all advertised as 'free'. For most users, this seems like an obvious bargain. Yet running these services is far from cheap, and the companies behind them are rarely charities. If we are not paying with money, it is worth asking what we are paying with instead.

The answer, in most cases, is data. Every time we use a free app, we generate information: the messages we send, the videos we watch, the places we visit, and even how long we pause on a particular image. On its own, a single piece of this data may seem trivial. Collected on a vast scale and combined cleverly, however, it builds a remarkably detailed picture of who we are—our habits, our interests, and our weaknesses.

This information is valuable because it can be used to sell us things. Advertisers will pay generously to show their messages to exactly the people most likely to buy. A company that knows you have been searching for running shoes can charge a sportswear brand a premium to place its advertisement in front of you at the perfect moment. In this sense, the user is not really the customer at all. The true customers are the advertisers, and the user's attention is the product being sold.

Defenders of this model point out that it has real benefits. Because advertising covers the costs, powerful tools are made available to billions of people who could never afford to pay for them. A teenager in any country can use the same software as a wealthy professional. Targeted advertising, they add, is at least more relevant than the random commercials of the past.

Critics, however, raise serious concerns. They argue that few users truly understand how much information they are giving away, or how long it will be kept. Once collected, personal data can be leaked, sold to third parties, or used to influence opinions in ways that are difficult to detect. There is also the question of attention itself: many apps are deliberately designed to be addictive, since the longer we stay, the more data and advertising revenue they produce.

None of this means that free apps are simply a trap to be avoided. They are now woven into modern life, and many are genuinely useful. The point is rather that 'free' is a misleading word. Understanding the real exchange taking place allows us to make more thoughtful choices—about which apps deserve our trust, and about how much of ourselves we are willing to hand over.

questions.stem: What is the main idea of the passage?
options: 'Free' apps are paid for with users' personal data rather than with money.
Online advertising is harmful to absolutely everyone.
Free apps are always a trap that should be avoided.
All apps ought to charge users a small monthly fee.

readingLong.title: How the Printing Press Changed the World
readingLong.passage:
For most of human history, books were rare and precious objects. Before the fifteenth century, every book in Europe had to be copied out by hand, usually by monks working slowly in monasteries. A single Bible might take a scribe more than a year to complete. Because the work was so slow, books were extremely expensive, and only the rich, the church, and a few universities could own them. Most ordinary people never held a book in their lives.

This situation changed dramatically thanks to a German craftsman named Johannes Gutenberg. Around 1440, he developed a printing press that used small metal letters, known as 'movable type'. These letters could be arranged to form a page, inked, and pressed onto paper. When one page had been printed many times, the letters could be rearranged to make a completely different page. For the first time, identical copies of a text could be produced quickly and in large numbers.

The effect was enormous. A task that had once taken months could now be done in days. As printing spread across Europe, the price of books fell sharply, and the number of books in circulation grew from a few thousand to many millions within fifty years. Ideas that had previously reached only a handful of scholars could now travel far and wide.

This flood of printed material had consequences that Gutenberg could never have imagined. Because books became affordable, more people had a reason to learn to read, and literacy slowly increased. New scientific discoveries could be shared between countries instead of remaining hidden in a single library. Religious and political ideas spread rapidly, sometimes challenging those in power. Some historians argue that without the printing press, the great movements that reshaped Europe in the following centuries would have been impossible.

The press also changed the way knowledge itself was treated. When every copy of a book had been slightly different, mistakes easily crept in as texts were copied again and again. Printed books, by contrast, were identical, which made it easier to correct errors and to refer precisely to a particular page. Knowledge became something that could be built up, shared, and checked by many people at once.

Today, in an age of instant digital communication, it is easy to forget how revolutionary the printing press once was. Yet the basic idea behind it—that information should be cheap, accurate, and available to ordinary people—still shapes the modern world. Gutenberg's invention did not merely make books; it helped create the very idea of a reading public.

readingLong.title: The True Cost of Fast Fashion
readingLong.passage:
Walk into many clothing shops today and you will find shelves full of cheap, fashionable garments that change almost every week. A T-shirt may cost less than a sandwich, and a whole new outfit can be bought for the price of a cinema ticket. This way of producing and selling clothes quickly and cheaply is known as 'fast fashion'. It has made stylish clothing available to almost everyone, but it also carries hidden costs that are only now becoming clear.

The first cost is environmental. Making clothes uses enormous amounts of water and energy. Producing a single cotton T-shirt, for example, can require around two and a half thousand litres of water—roughly what one person drinks in three years. The dyes used to colour fabrics often end up in rivers, and the synthetic materials in cheap clothing can take hundreds of years to break down. Because fast fashion encourages people to buy far more than they need, mountains of barely worn clothes are thrown away each year, much of it ending up in landfill or being burned.

There is also a human cost. To keep prices low, many garments are made in factories where workers are paid very little and may work long hours in unsafe conditions. The pressure to produce clothes faster and more cheaply can mean that the people who actually sew them earn barely enough to live on. Customers in wealthy countries are often completely unaware of the circumstances in which their bargains were made.

Defenders of the industry argue that it provides jobs in poorer regions and allows people on low incomes to dress well. These points are not without merit. For many families, a factory wage, however small, is better than no work at all. Yet critics reply that decent pay and safe conditions should not be treated as luxuries, and that cheap prices in one part of the world should not depend on suffering in another.

Fortunately, there are signs of change. Some shoppers are choosing to buy fewer, better-made items that last longer, a habit sometimes called 'slow fashion'. Others buy second-hand clothes or repair what they already own. A number of companies have begun to use recycled materials and to be more open about how their products are made. Governments, too, are beginning to consider rules that would force brands to take responsibility for the waste they create.

None of these solutions is complete on its own. Still, they all point in the same direction: towards a future in which the real price of our clothes—paid by the planet and by other people—is no longer kept conveniently out of sight.

readingLong.title: Why Do We Dream?
readingLong.passage:
Every night, as we sleep, our minds create strange and vivid stories that we call dreams. We may find ourselves flying over cities, talking to people we have never met, or sitting an exam we did not study for. Most dreams are forgotten within minutes of waking, yet the question of why we dream at all has fascinated people for thousands of years. Today, scientists still cannot agree on a single answer, but they have developed several interesting theories.

To understand dreaming, researchers first had to understand sleep. During the night, the brain passes through several stages, one of which is called REM sleep, short for 'rapid eye movement'. During this stage, the eyes move quickly behind closed lids, the body becomes still, and brain activity rises to levels almost as high as when we are awake. It is mainly during REM sleep that our most memorable dreams occur.

One popular theory suggests that dreaming helps us deal with emotions. According to this view, the sleeping brain replays difficult or stressful experiences in a safe setting, allowing us to process them. This might explain why people often dream about events that worry them, and why a good night's sleep can make a problem seem less frightening in the morning.

A second theory focuses on memory. Throughout the day, we take in huge amounts of information, but not all of it is worth keeping. Some scientists believe that during sleep the brain sorts through these memories, strengthening the important ones and discarding the rest. Dreams, in this view, may simply be a side effect of this nightly tidying process.

Others take a very different position. They argue that dreams have no real purpose at all. During REM sleep, they say, the brain stem sends out random signals, and the higher parts of the brain do their best to weave these signals into a story. The result feels meaningful, but it is essentially the brain making sense of noise.

It is quite possible that more than one of these theories is correct. Dreaming may help with emotions and memory while also being partly random. What seems increasingly clear is that sleep, and the dreaming that accompanies it, is far from a waste of time. People who are repeatedly woken during REM sleep become anxious and find it harder to concentrate, as though something necessary has been taken from them.

For now, dreams remain one of the mind's great mysteries. We spend years of our lives in this strange nightly world, and although science has begun to light the way, much about why we dream is still waiting to be discovered.

readingLong.title: The Search for Life Beyond Earth
readingLong.passage:
Are we alone in the universe? This is one of the oldest and biggest questions that humans have ever asked. For most of history, it could only be answered with guesses and stories. In recent decades, however, science has begun to search for an answer in a serious, organised way. The study of possible life beyond our own planet is known as astrobiology.

Scientists looking for life elsewhere usually start by asking what life needs. On Earth, every living thing requires liquid water, a source of energy such as sunlight, and certain chemical building blocks. Rather than searching for aliens directly, researchers therefore look for places where these conditions might exist. This approach is sometimes summed up in the phrase 'follow the water'.

Within our own solar system, a few places look surprisingly promising. Mars, for example, was once warmer and wetter than it is today, and dried-up riverbeds suggest that water flowed across its surface long ago. Some of the moons of Jupiter and Saturn are even more interesting. Beneath their icy surfaces, scientists believe there may be vast oceans of liquid water, kept warm by heat from inside the moons. If life can survive in the dark, cold oceans of Earth, perhaps it could survive there too.

The search does not stop at our solar system. Using powerful telescopes, astronomers have now discovered thousands of planets orbiting other stars, known as exoplanets. A small number of these lie in what is called the 'habitable zone'—the region around a star where temperatures would allow liquid water to exist. Finding such planets does not prove that they contain life, but it does show that Earth-like worlds are not as rare as once feared.

Even if simple life is common, however, finding it will not be easy. Most exoplanets are so far away that we cannot visit them or even see them clearly. Instead, scientists hope to study the light that passes through their atmospheres. Certain gases, if found together, might be a sign that living things are present. Detecting these faint signals across enormous distances is an enormous technical challenge.

It is important to remember that no firm evidence of life beyond Earth has yet been found. The discovery of even the simplest alien microbe would be one of the greatest in the history of science, and researchers are careful not to claim too much too soon. Still, the search continues with growing confidence. Whatever the final answer turns out to be, it is likely to change forever the way we think about our place in the universe.

options: Aliens have already been discovered on Mars.

readingLong.title: Why English Became a Global Language
readingLong.passage:
Today, English is spoken by roughly a quarter of the world's population, though only a minority of those speakers learned it as their first language. It is the main language of international business, science, aviation, and the internet. A Japanese engineer and a Brazilian designer meeting at a conference in Germany will, more often than not, conduct their conversation in English. How did a language once confined to a small island come to occupy such a position?

It is tempting to assume that English succeeded because of some quality within the language itself—that it is unusually simple, logical, or beautiful. Linguists are firmly united in rejecting this idea. English grammar contains plenty of irregularities, its spelling is notoriously inconsistent, and its pronunciation puzzles learners and native speakers alike. No serious scholar believes that English spread because it was easier than its rivals. The real explanation lies not in the language but in history.

The story begins with the British Empire. From the seventeenth century onwards, Britain established colonies, trading posts, and settlements across the globe, from North America to India to Australia. Wherever British power reached, the English language followed, becoming the language of administration, law, and education. By the time the empire declined in the twentieth century, English had already taken root on every inhabited continent.

The second decisive factor was the rise of the United States. As Britain's influence faded, America emerged as the world's leading economic, military, and cultural power. American films, music, and, later, computer technology carried English into homes everywhere. A language already widespread because of one empire was thus reinforced by the dominance of another nation that happened to share it.

Once a language reaches a certain point, its spread tends to accelerate for a simple reason: usefulness. The more people who speak English, the more valuable it becomes to learn, since it allows communication with an ever-larger number of others. Parents want their children to study it; businesses demand it of their employees; universities teach their courses in it. Success, in this sense, breeds further success.

This dominance brings real advantages. A shared language allows ideas to cross borders and lets people from utterly different backgrounds cooperate. Yet it also raises concerns. As English advances, smaller languages can be pushed aside, and with them go unique ways of seeing the world. Native English speakers, meanwhile, may enjoy unfair advantages while feeling little pressure to learn the languages of others.

The position of English is not guaranteed forever. Languages have risen and fallen before, and the global balance of power continues to shift. For the present, however, English remains the closest thing the world has ever had to a common tongue—a status it owes far more to historical accident than to any virtue of its own.

questions.stem: What is the main idea of the passage?
options: Smaller languages are disappearing very quickly.
English is by far the easiest language to learn.
English is certain to remain dominant forever.
English became a global language mainly because of historical events, not its own qualities.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — ใจความหลัก: อังกฤษเป็นภาษาโลกเพราะปัจจัยทางประวัติศาสตร์ ไม่ใช่คุณสมบัติของภาษาเอง

questions.stem: What do linguists say about the idea that English succeeded because it is simple?
options: They believe it is mostly true.
They have never studied the question.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — 'Linguists are firmly united in rejecting this idea'

questions.stem: According to the passage, how did the British Empire help spread English?
options: It printed millions of English dictionaries.
It made English the language of administration, law, and education in its colonies.
It banned all other empires from trading.
It forced everyone to forget their own language.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — 'becoming the language of administration, law, and education'

questions.stem: What was the second decisive factor in the spread of English?
options: The rise of the United States as a leading world power.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — 'The second decisive factor was the rise of the United States'

questions.stem: In paragraph 5, the word "accelerate" is closest in meaning to
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — 'its spread tends to accelerate' = เร็วขึ้น

questions.stem: Why does the writer say that "success breeds further success"?
options: The more people speak English, the more useful—and so more popular—it becomes.
English is taught only to successful people.
English is steadily becoming less common.
Success has nothing to do with language.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ยิ่งคนพูดอังกฤษมาก ยิ่งคุ้มที่จะเรียน → ยิ่งแพร่หลายขึ้นไปอีก

questions.stem: In the last sentence, "any virtue of its own" refers to a supposed good quality of
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — เทียบว่าอังกฤษได้สถานะนี้เพราะประวัติศาสตร์ ไม่ใช่ 'virtue of its own' = คุณสมบัติดีของตัวภาษาอังกฤษ

questions.stem: What is the writer's attitude?
options: Analytical, weighing both benefits and concerns.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — 'brings real advantages ... Yet it also raises concerns' → วิเคราะห์ทั้งข้อดีข้อเสีย

questions.stem: How is the passage mainly organised?
options: It tells the life story of one English teacher.
It lists English words by their difficulty.
It rejects one explanation, then gives the historical reasons, and finally weighs the effects.
It compares two foreign languages in detail.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — ปฏิเสธคำอธิบายหนึ่ง → เหตุผลเชิงประวัติศาสตร์ → ชั่งน้ำหนักผลกระทบ

readingLong.title: Why Music Moves Us
readingLong.passage:
There is no known human society, past or present, that lacks music. Long before people could write, they were singing, drumming, and making instruments from bone and wood. Music accompanies our weddings and our funerals, our celebrations and our worship. It can bring a crowd of strangers to tears or fill them with sudden joy. Yet precisely how an arrangement of sounds achieves such powerful effects remains, in many ways, a mystery that scientists and philosophers are still working to solve.

Part of the answer seems to lie in expectation. As we listen to a piece of music, our brains constantly predict what will come next, drawing on patterns we have heard before. A skilful composer plays with these predictions, sometimes fulfilling them and sometimes delaying or breaking them. When a long-awaited note finally arrives, or a melody resolves after a moment of tension, the brain rewards us with a small rush of pleasure. Much of the emotional force of music, researchers suggest, comes from this interplay between expectation and surprise.

Music also appears to be deeply connected to movement. Rhythm, in particular, has a remarkable power to make us want to tap our feet, nod our heads, or dance. Some scientists believe this link reflects the way music evolved alongside group activities such as marching, working, and dancing together. Moving in time with others may once have helped early human groups cooperate and bond, which could explain why shared musical experiences still feel so unifying today.

Then there is the relationship between music and memory. A song heard in childhood can, decades later, bring back not only the tune but the smells, faces, and feelings of a particular moment. This is why a few familiar bars can transport an elderly listener vividly into the past. Doctors have even used music to reach patients whose memories have otherwise faded, sometimes prompting responses that ordinary conversation cannot.

It would be a mistake, however, to reduce music to a set of psychological tricks. The meaning of a piece of music is shaped as much by culture as by biology. A scale that sounds cheerful in one tradition may sound mournful in another, and a rhythm that excites one audience may seem ordinary to people who grew up surrounded by it. What music expresses, and how it is understood, depends heavily on where and when one happens to live.

Perhaps this combination of the universal and the personal is exactly what makes music so compelling. It reaches into something shared by all human beings, yet it speaks to each of us in a private language shaped by our own lives. We may never fully explain why a particular song can move us so deeply. For most listeners, though, the experience itself is reason enough to keep listening.

questions.stem: What is the main idea of the passage?
options: Music affects us deeply for reasons that science is still trying to fully explain.
Only trained musicians can truly enjoy music.
All cultures hear music in exactly the same way.
Music has no real effect on memory.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — ใจความหลัก: ดนตรีส่งผลต่อเราอย่างลึกซึ้งด้วยเหตุผลที่วิทยาศาสตร์ยังอธิบายไม่หมด

questions.stem: According to the passage, where does much of music's emotional force come from?
options: The sheer loudness of the sounds.
The interplay between expectation and surprise.
The age of the instruments used.
The language of the song's lyrics.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ B — 'the emotional force of music ... comes from this interplay between expectation and surprise'

questions.stem: Why might music be linked to movement?
options: It may have evolved alongside group activities like marching and dancing.
Moving the body makes music louder.
Rhythm has no real effect on the body.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — 'music evolved alongside group activities such as marching, working, and dancing together'

questions.stem: How have doctors used music with some patients?
options: To reach patients whose memories have faded.
To teach patients how to read.
To put restless patients to sleep.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — 'used music to reach patients whose memories have otherwise faded'

questions.stem: In the final paragraph, the word "compelling" is closest in meaning to
options: powerfully attractive and hard to resist.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — 'what makes music so compelling' = ดึงดูดใจอย่างแรงจนยากจะต้านทาน

questions.stem: What does the example of a scale that sounds cheerful in one tradition but mournful in another show?
options: Musical scales have no emotional effect.
The meaning of music depends partly on culture.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ D — ความหมายของดนตรีขึ้นกับวัฒนธรรมด้วย ไม่ใช่แค่ชีววิทยา

questions.stem: In paragraph 4, "a particular moment" is connected with
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ C — 'A song heard in childhood can ... bring back ... the feelings of a particular moment'

questions.stem: What is the writer's main purpose?
options: To explore the different reasons why music affects people so strongly.
To teach readers how to play an instrument.
To argue that one type of music is best.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — สำรวจเหตุผลหลายด้านว่าทำไมดนตรีจึงกระทบใจเราอย่างมาก

questions.stem: How is the passage mainly organised?
options: By examining several possible explanations, then noting the role of culture.
By telling the story of one famous song.
By comparing the lives of two composers.
By listing instruments from oldest to newest.
questions.explanation_th: ตอบข้อ A — พิจารณาคำอธิบายหลายข้อ (ความคาดหวัง/การเคลื่อนไหว/ความจำ) → แล้วชี้บทบาทของวัฒนธรรม

readingLong.title: The Hidden Cost of Fast Fashion
readingLong.passage:
The contemporary apparel industry has undergone a paradigm shift over the past two decades, dominated largely by the phenomenon known as "fast fashion." This business model is predicated on rapidly translating high fashion design trends into low-priced garments, allowing consumers to purchase trendy clothing at a fraction of traditional costs. While this approach has democratized fashion, enabling individuals across various socioeconomic backgrounds to participate in global trends, its underlying economic and environmental ramifications are drawing intense scrutiny from economists and environmentalists alike.

Proponents of fast fashion frequently highlight its economic contributions. The industry generates billions of dollars in revenue annually and provides employment for millions of workers in developing nations. By keeping production costs minimal, fast fashion brands stimulate continuous consumer spending, which theoretically bolsters the global retail economy. However, critics argue that this economic boom is largely a facade built upon unsustainable practices. The relentless drive to minimize costs often results in depressed wages and hazardous working conditions in manufacturing hubs. Consequently, the apparent economic benefit to the consumer is subsidized by the exploitation of laborers overseas.

Furthermore, the environmental footprint of fast fashion represents a massive deferred economic cost. The industry is responsible for nearly ten percent of global carbon emissions and is the second-largest consumer of the world's water supply. The synthetic fibers commonly used, such as polyester, do not easily degrade, leading to millions of tons of textile waste languishing in landfills each year. Eventually, governments—and by extension, taxpayers—must bear the exorbitant costs of managing this waste and mitigating the associated environmental degradation.

In response, a counter-movement advocating for "sustainable fashion" has gained traction. Sustainable brands prioritize ethical labor practices and environmentally friendly materials. Admittedly, the initial retail price of sustainable garments is significantly higher, deterring price-sensitive consumers. Nevertheless, advocates emphasize the concept of "cost per wear." A durable, sustainably made shirt may cost three times as much as its fast fashion counterpart, but it is designed to last exponentially longer.

Some policymakers are now proposing "extended producer responsibility" (EPR) legislation. Under such laws, clothing manufacturers would be legally mandated to fund the recycling or safe disposal of their products at the end of their lifecycle. If implemented, these regulations would inherently force fast fashion brands to internalize their waste management costs, inevitably driving up the retail prices of their garments and making sustainable options comparatively more competitive. Transitioning the global market requires moving away from the gratification of disposable clothing toward valuing longevity. Until then, the true cost of a five-dollar t-shirt will remain hidden, paid for by the planet and future generations.

questions.stem: What is the primary purpose of the passage?
options: To analyze the hidden costs and impacts of the fast fashion industry.
To persuade readers to completely boycott all fast fashion brands.
To explain the manufacturing process of synthetic clothing fibers.
To praise the economic benefits that fast fashion brings to developing nations.
questions.explanation_th: บทความวิเคราะห์ทั้งผลกระทบทางเศรษฐกิจแฝงและสิ่งแวดล้อมของ fast fashion อย่างสมดุล ไม่ได้เชียร์ฝ่ายใดฝ่ายหนึ่งสุดโต่ง

questions.stem: According to paragraph 1, how has fast fashion "democratized" the industry?
options: By making trendy clothing affordable for people of different income levels.
By giving factory workers the right to vote on their wages.
By forcing high-end designers to lower their prices.
By allowing consumers to design their own cheap garments.
questions.explanation_th: 'enabling individuals across various socioeconomic backgrounds to participate' + 'purchase trendy clothing at a fraction of traditional costs' = คนทุกระดับฐานะเข้าถึงแฟชั่นได้

questions.stem: The author uses the word "facade" in paragraph 2 to suggest that the economic success of fast fashion is ________.
questions.explanation_th: facade แปลว่า ฉากหน้า/สิ่งลวงตา สื่อว่าความสำเร็จทางเศรษฐกิจนี้ไม่ใช่เรื่องจริงทั้งหมด

questions.stem: Who ultimately pays the environmental costs of textile waste, according to the passage?
options: The factory workers in developing nations
questions.explanation_th: 'governments—and by extension, taxpayers—must bear the exorbitant costs'

questions.stem: What does the concept of "cost per wear" imply?
options: An expensive but durable item might be a better financial choice in the long run.
Consumers should only buy clothes that they wear on a daily basis.
Sustainable brands overcharge customers for basic items.
Fast fashion is actually cheaper no matter how long the clothes last.
questions.explanation_th: เสื้อที่แพงกว่าแต่ทนกว่า ('designed to last exponentially longer') อาจคุ้มค่ากว่าในระยะยาว

questions.stem: What is the main goal of "extended producer responsibility" (EPR) legislation?
options: To make manufacturers financially responsible for the disposal of their garments.
To force companies to pay their overseas workers a higher minimum wage.
To ban the use of synthetic materials like polyester in clothing.
To give consumers a tax break for purchasing sustainable clothing.
questions.explanation_th: 'legally mandated to fund the recycling or safe disposal of their products'

questions.stem: Which of the following best describes the author's attitude toward the five-dollar t-shirt?
questions.explanation_th: ผู้เขียนวิจารณ์ว่าต้นทุนที่แท้จริงถูกผลักภาระให้ 'the planet and future generations' โดยไม่เปิดเผย

questions.stem: The word "internalize" in the final paragraph is closest in meaning to ________.
options: to absorb and take direct responsibility for something
to transfer the cost to someone else
to ignore or overlook a problem
questions.explanation_th: internalize = รับภาระ/ทำให้เป็นต้นทุนของตนเอง (ตรงข้ามกับ externalize ที่แปลว่าผลักภาระให้ผู้อื่น) — บริบท 'force fast fashion brands to internalize their waste management costs' คือบังคับให้แบรนด์รับผิดชอบต้นทุนกำจัดขยะเอง

readingLong.title: The Cashless Society Debate
readingLong.passage:
Over the past decade, the global financial landscape has been steadily migrating toward a cashless society, a paradigm where physical currency—banknotes and coins—is entirely replaced by digital transactions. Driven by rapid advancements in mobile payment technology and the widespread adoption of contactless credit cards, this transition promises unprecedented convenience and efficiency. However, while proponents celebrate the modernization of commerce, critics caution that the rush to eliminate cash introduces profound socioeconomic and privacy concerns that remain largely unaddressed.

From an economic standpoint, the advantages of a cashless system are compelling. Digital transactions significantly reduce the logistical costs associated with printing, transporting, and securing physical money. Furthermore, proponents argue that eliminating cash can severely curtail illicit activities, such as tax evasion, money laundering, and the black-market drug trade, because digital footprints are inherently traceable by law enforcement agencies. For the average consumer, the appeal lies in frictionless commerce; purchasing goods requires merely a tap of a smartphone, streamlining the retail experience and eliminating the need to carry cumbersome wallets.

Despite these benefits, the complete eradication of physical currency poses a severe threat to marginalized populations. A fully digital economy operates on the assumption that every citizen has reliable access to banking services, smartphones, and stable internet connections. In reality, millions of low-income individuals, elderly citizens, and rural residents remain "unbanked." For these demographic groups, cash is not merely a preference but a vital necessity for day-to-day survival. Transitioning to an exclusively digital framework risks financially disenfranchising these vulnerable populations, effectively locking them out of the modern economy.

Moreover, privacy advocates raise substantial ethical questions regarding data sovereignty. In a cashless society, every financial transaction—from buying a morning coffee to purchasing medical supplies—is recorded, analyzed, and stored by third-party financial institutions. This wealth of personal data can be monetized by corporations for targeted advertising or, more alarmingly, monitored by authoritarian governments to track the behavior of their citizens. The anonymity that physical cash provides is entirely stripped away in a digital ecosystem, leaving individuals vulnerable to surveillance and data breaches. Instances of widespread identity theft and centralized system outages further highlight the fragility of relying solely on digital networks.

Ultimately, while the technological momentum toward digital payments is undeniable, completely abandoning cash is a premature endeavor. Policymakers must implement robust regulatory safeguards to protect consumer privacy and ensure that alternative, non-digital payment methods remain legally mandated for essential services. Furthermore, educational campaigns must be funded to bridge the digital literacy gap among older generations. A truly progressive financial system should leverage the efficiency of digital innovation without sacrificing the inclusivity and autonomy historically afforded by physical currency. Until a universally accessible digital infrastructure is guaranteed, cash must remain an integral pillar of the economy.

questions.stem: What is the primary purpose of the passage?
options: To evaluate the benefits and drawbacks of moving toward a cashless society.
To persuade readers to stop using credit cards immediately.
To explain the technical mechanisms of mobile payment apps.
To argue that physical cash is the only secure way to manage finances.
questions.explanation_th: บทความประเมินทั้งข้อดีและข้อเสียของสังคมไร้เงินสดอย่างสมดุล

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, how does a cashless society help combat crime?
options: By creating trackable digital records of all financial activities.
By automatically freezing the bank accounts of suspected criminals.
By making it impossible to buy illegal drugs online.
By hiring more law enforcement officers to monitor transactions.
questions.explanation_th: digital footprints ที่ตรวจสอบได้ช่วยลดอาชญากรรมทางการเงิน

questions.stem: What does the author mean by "frictionless commerce" in the second paragraph?
options: Easy and seamless buying processes without physical delays.
Shopping experiences that involve heavy negotiations.
Transactions that are completely free of tax and extra fees.
The physical exchange of goods without using any currency.
questions.explanation_th: frictionless commerce หมายถึงการซื้อขายที่ราบรื่น รวดเร็ว ไม่ติดขัด

questions.stem: Who would be most negatively affected by a purely digital economy?
questions.explanation_th: กลุ่มที่ไม่มีบัญชีธนาคารและผู้สูงอายุจะได้รับผลกระทบหนักที่สุด

questions.stem: Why do privacy advocates oppose the elimination of cash?
options: It forces citizens to surrender their transactional anonymity.
It makes it easier for people to evade their annual taxes.
It increases the cost of printing physical banknotes.
It requires everyone to purchase expensive smartphones.
questions.explanation_th: การไม่เปิดเผยตัวตนที่เงินสดให้ไว้จะหายไปในระบบดิจิทัล

questions.stem: Which word is closest in meaning to "disenfranchising" in paragraph 3?
questions.explanation_th: disenfranchising แปลว่า ลิดรอนสิทธิ์/ผลักให้เป็นชายขอบ

questions.stem: What does the author suggest policymakers should do?
options: Ensure physical cash is still accepted for essential services.
Completely ban the use of mobile payment technologies.
Force all citizens to open digital bank accounts immediately.
Tax digital transactions heavily to discourage their use.
questions.explanation_th: ผู้เขียนเสนอให้รับรองว่าบริการที่จำเป็นยังคงรับเงินสดได้

questions.stem: What is the author's overall stance on the transition to a cashless society?
options: Cautious, acknowledging its benefits but emphasizing its severe risks.
Fully supportive, as it represents inevitable progress.
Completely opposed, viewing it as a technological failure.
Indifferent, believing it will not affect the average person.
questions.explanation_th: ผู้เขียนยอมรับข้อดีแต่เน้นย้ำความเสี่ยงที่ยังไม่ได้รับการแก้ไข ไม่ได้เชียร์สุดโต่งฝั่งใดฝั่งหนึ่ง
